title: Epic: User & Authentication Foundation
description: Deliver complete user identity and authentication foundation for Anizaki, covering register, login/logout, forgot password, profile update, email verification, and role-based authorization (user/seller/admin) across backend, frontend, tests, and docs.
design: This epic establishes the first production-grade identity and authorization capability for Anizaki. It extends the current clean-architecture scaffold into a complete auth foundation shared by backend and frontend.
acceptance_criteria: All six user stories are implemented end-to-end with automated verification for success and failure paths; role-based access for user/seller/admin is enforced consistently; docs and runbooks are updated for future contributors.
notes: Rationale: auth is a cross-cutting prerequisite for later business modules. Scope explicitly excludes social login/MFA to keep delivery bounded while ensuring future extensibility through clear abstractions.
close_reason: User-auth foundation delivered end-to-end; remaining bd-12f.10* items are deferred hardening backlog

title: Epic: Search & Filter Library Drawings
description: Deliver deterministic search, category filter, user-controlled sort, and pagination for the Library drawings list so users can find the right drawing and open its detail page without losing context.
design: This epic upgrades the existing Library read-only slice instead of replacing it. The key design constraint is a single query semantics that remains aligned across URL state, React state, frontend transport, API validation, and repository execution.
acceptance_criteria: Search/category/sort/pagination work together end-to-end; direct query-string URLs reconstruct state; invalid sort inputs are handled safely and consistently; related Library tests and verification commands pass.
notes: Context: created after full AGENTS/README/code investigation on 2026-03-12. The workspace already has /library list/category/detail routes and an in-memory repository; the missing capability is user-controlled sort and the surrounding hardening needed to keep the list flow deterministic and shareable.

title: Phase 1: Search/filter/sort semantics baseline
description: Lock the canonical semantics for search, category filter, sort, pagination, deep-linking, and invalid-query behavior before implementation fans out across frontend and backend.
design: Architecture-first planning track. This phase exists to prevent drift between frontend normalization and backend validation, which is the highest risk when adding query-state features on an existing vertical slice.
acceptance_criteria: Canonical semantics, reset rules, deterministic ordering rules, and implementation sequencing notes are explicit enough that downstream implementation beads can proceed without inventing their own behavior.
notes: Why this matters: epic scope looks small on the surface, but the real risk is silent inconsistency across layers. Future contributors should treat this phase as the semantic source of truth for the epic.

title: Define canonical query semantics for search/filter/sort/page
description: Specify the authoritative meaning of page, pageSize, category, search, sortBy, and sortDir for the Library drawings list, including the default sort state and the combinations that must be supported together.
design: This task produces the semantic contract that both frontend and backend will implement. It should answer behavior questions before code is changed so that every downstream bead can stay small and reversible.
acceptance_criteria: Defaults, allowed sort values, combination rules, and reset expectations are clear enough to drive implementation and tests without guesswork.
notes: Rationale: the current code already supports page/pageSize/category/search. Adding sort without a canonical contract would encourage each layer to improvise its own behavior.
close_reason: Completed by Sisyphus

title: Define URL normalization and invalid-query fallback policy
description: Document how URL query parameters are parsed, normalized, omitted, preserved, or rejected across the list flow, with an explicit distinction between user-facing malformed deep links and direct API misuse for sortBy/sortDir.
design: This task separates frontend resilience from backend strictness. The optimized product stance is: malformed shared links on /library should recover to the canonical safe default state for end users, while direct API callers still receive strict validation_failed responses for unsupported sort values.
acceptance_criteria: Deep-link rules, default omission rules, and invalid-value handling are explicit enough for query-utils tests, page-behavior tests, and API validation tests to target directly; the user-facing route fallback policy and direct API rejection policy are no longer ambiguous.
notes: Optimization pass (2026-03-12): raised to P0 because this policy directly shapes user experience and resolves a likely implementation conflict between 'shareable URL resilience' and 'strict API contract validation'. Future contributors should preserve: safe fallback on malformed /library URLs, strict 400 validation_failed on invalid API sort inputs.
close_reason: Completed by Sisyphus

title: Define deterministic ordering and pagination stability rule
description: Pin down the deterministic ordering rule for all supported sort modes, including the stable secondary key to use when multiple drawings share the same primary sort value.
design: This task guards against one of the most subtle failure modes in paginated lists: items shifting positions unpredictably across loads or pages. Repository execution and tests should consume this rule directly.
acceptance_criteria: Primary and secondary ordering rules are explicit for createdAt, title, and code sorts, and downstream repository/tests can verify stability against them.
notes: Even with the current in-memory dataset, locking this rule now prevents future storage migrations from silently changing user-visible behavior.

title: Record implementation touch-map and sequencing notes
description: Capture the concrete file touchpoints, sequencing assumptions, and boundary considerations needed to implement the epic with small diffs across frontend, backend, and tests.
design: This is the bridge between semantic planning and execution. It keeps future implementation work aligned with existing project boundaries and reduces random edits across the monorepo.
acceptance_criteria: The file-touch map and sequencing notes are concrete enough that future implementers can tell where the epic should and should not change code.
notes: Why this exists: the repo already has clean boundaries and feature-based structure. Preserving those boundaries is part of ?done,? not a nice-to-have.

title: Phase 2: Frontend query-state and transport
description: Extend frontend query utilities and API transport so sort state can be parsed from the URL, preserved in page state, serialized back into the URL, and sent to the backend contract correctly.
design: Frontend contract track focused on state normalization and transport correctness. The output of this phase is not the visible UI control alone, but a trustworthy query model that every page-level interaction can reuse.
acceptance_criteria: Frontend query model supports sortBy/sortDir defaults and normalization, API wrappers transport sort correctly, and dedicated tests protect parsing/serialization/transport behavior.
notes: This phase intentionally starts below the page component level. If query utilities and transport are wrong, UI work later will only make the defect harder to see.

title: Extend libraryQueryUtils for sort state and defaults
description: Expand the Library query-state utilities to model sortBy/sortDir, normalize supported values, apply safe defaults, and map the normalized state into API params and URL query strings.
design: This task is the frontend foundation for the epic. Query utilities should remain the single place where list-state normalization logic lives so page components stay thin and predictable.
acceptance_criteria: LibraryListQueryState includes sort fields, parse/toApiParams/toQueryString/defaultQueryState reflect the new contract, and invalid sort inputs normalize safely to the canonical defaults.
notes: Current evidence: libraryQueryUtils only handles page/pageSize/category/search. This task turns it into the canonical frontend state model for the full list feature.

title: Protect query parsing and serialization with unit tests
description: Add or update unit tests to lock parsing, normalization, default omission, serialization, and invalid-input fallback behavior for the expanded library query-state utilities.
design: This test task exists to protect semantic correctness, not just code coverage. The main goal is to catch silent drift in URL behavior before it reaches page-level UI tests.
acceptance_criteria: Tests cover valid sort parsing, default fallback, query-string serialization, omission of default params, and normalization of invalid inputs.
notes: These tests are the fastest feedback loop for the epic. They should be treated as the frontline guard against accidental query-state regressions.

title: Extend frontend libraryApi contract with sort transport
description: Update frontend library API wrappers so getDrawingList can accept sortBy/sortDir and include them correctly in requests to /api/v1/library/drawings.
design: Transport should stay dumb and faithful to the normalized query model. The wrapper must reflect the backend contract exactly so the page layer does not need to know about low-level query assembly.
acceptance_criteria: GetDrawingListParams includes sortBy/sortDir, the wrapper passes them through correctly, and default behavior does not create malformed query payloads.
notes: This task is where frontend and backend contract drift becomes visible earliest. Keeping the wrapper explicit is safer than letting pages build query objects ad hoc.

title: Add frontend API wrapper tests for sort transport
description: Add tests that prove the library API wrapper sends sortBy/sortDir correctly and does not generate incorrect query payloads when sort is omitted.
design: This task gives the FE-BE transport layer its own contract check so page behavior tests do not have to diagnose low-level query bugs.
acceptance_criteria: Wrapper tests verify sort params are forwarded when present and that omission cases remain clean and predictable.
notes: This is intentionally a narrow contract test. It should fail loudly if future contributors change wrapper behavior without updating semantics.

title: Phase 3: Backend sort contract and deterministic execution
description: Extend the backend query contract, validation, endpoint parsing, handler flow, and repository implementation so API consumers can request deterministic sorting for the library drawings list.
design: Backend contract track focused on making sort a real API capability rather than a frontend-only state. Deterministic repository ordering is the critical safeguard because pagination quality depends on it.
acceptance_criteria: GetDrawingList accepts valid sortBy/sortDir values, rejects invalid ones with validation_failed, repository execution honors sort selection with stable ordering, and backend tests cover valid and invalid branches.
notes: This phase keeps the in-memory repository as the storage boundary on purpose. The epic is about behavior and contract integrity, not storage migration.

title: Extend GetDrawingListQuery and validator for sort contract
description: Add sortBy/sortDir to the backend GetDrawingListQuery contract and extend validation rules so only supported values are accepted by the application layer.
design: Validation belongs close to the query contract so every entrypoint into the use case sees the same rules. This task is the backend mirror of the semantics locked in Phase 1.
acceptance_criteria: GetDrawingListQuery carries sort state, validator accepts only canonical sort values, and invalid sort inputs produce the project-standard validation failure path.
notes: Current evidence: the query contract only knows about page/pageSize/category/search. This task turns sort into a first-class backend concern.

title: Parse sort params in /api/v1/library/drawings endpoint
description: Extend the /api/v1/library/drawings endpoint so it reads sortBy/sortDir from query parameters, applies the same trimming/null handling style as existing filters, and constructs the expanded GetDrawingListQuery.
design: Endpoint parsing should remain thin and consistent with the rest of Program.cs. Its job is to bridge HTTP query-string input into the application contract without inventing new semantics.
acceptance_criteria: The list endpoint accepts sortBy/sortDir query params, maps them into GetDrawingListQuery, and relies on the validator for invalid-value rejection.
notes: This task is intentionally narrow because the repo already uses minimal APIs with lean endpoint lambdas. The endpoint should not absorb repository logic.

title: Extend repository contract and handler to carry sort state
description: Update ILibraryRepository and GetDrawingListHandler so the selected sort fields flow from the validated query contract into repository execution without breaking existing pagination and filter behavior.
design: The handler should stay orchestration-only while the repository remains responsible for actual ordering. This task keeps the clean-architecture seam intact as behavior expands.
acceptance_criteria: Handler passes validated sort state into ILibraryRepository, pagination metadata still computes correctly, and no existing filter/search behavior is lost in the transition.
notes: This seam matters for future persistence work. Keeping sort state explicit in the contract will make later repository swaps safer.

title: Implement deterministic sorting branches in InMemoryLibraryRepository
description: Implement sorting by createdAt, title, and code in InMemoryLibraryRepository, applying the agreed sort direction and a stable secondary key so paginated results remain deterministic.
design: Repository execution is the behavioral core of the epic. The implementation should stay simple and readable while making the ordering rules explicit enough that future data-store implementations can mirror them.
acceptance_criteria: Repository supports all canonical sort modes and directions, preserves search/filter behavior, and keeps pagination stable via deterministic secondary ordering.
notes: Current repository behavior is fixed ascending CreatedAtUtc ordering. This task is the point where the product capability becomes real.

title: Add application handler tests for sort and invalid input
description: Expand Library application tests to cover sorting branches, combined query scenarios, and validator rejection for invalid sortBy/sortDir values.
design: Application tests should prove the use case contract works independently of HTTP concerns. They are the best place to verify sorting semantics and combined query behavior at low cost.
acceptance_criteria: Handler tests cover createdAt/title/code sorting, asc/desc directions where relevant, combined search/category/sort/page behavior, and validation failures for bad sort values.
notes: The existing test suite already protects search/filter/pagination. This task extends that protection to the new axis of behavior without overloading API tests.

title: Add API integration tests for sort and validation failures
description: Expand end-to-end API tests for /api/v1/library/drawings so HTTP-level sorting, combined query behavior, and validation_failed responses are verified through the full request pipeline.
design: These tests exercise the full stack from routing to repository and are the final backend proof that public contract behavior matches the epic intent.
acceptance_criteria: API tests cover valid sort queries, invalid sortBy/sortDir failures, and combined search/category/sort/page responses with correct pagination metadata.
notes: Because the endpoint group is already established, this task should behave like contract hardening rather than exploratory implementation. Optimization pass (2026-03-12): API tests should verify strict validation_failed behavior for direct API misuse; they are intentionally not the place to define the frontend route's safe-fallback UX for malformed shared links.

title: Phase 4: Library list UX integration
description: Integrate sort controls and query behavior into the Library list UX so search, category filter, sort, pagination, empty-state handling, and detail navigation form one continuous user journey.
design: User-journey track focused on preserving context through every interaction. This phase should make the product feel more capable without destabilizing the route-based flow already present in the Library vertical slice.
acceptance_criteria: LibraryListPage exposes usable sort controls, preserves and resets query state correctly, keeps detail links intact, and page-level tests cover state persistence and page reset semantics.
notes: This is the most user-visible phase, but it depends heavily on Phases 2 and 3 being correct first. Treat category-page alignment as a deliberate review point, not an afterthought.

title: Add sort control to LibraryListPage
description: Add a clear sort control to LibraryListPage so users can choose the supported ordering modes without leaving the existing list flow.
design: The control should surface the current sort state and fit the existing page structure rather than introducing a new interaction model. Page code should delegate normalization to query utilities and transport to libraryApi.
acceptance_criteria: LibraryListPage renders a usable sort control, reflects the selected sort state, and uses the canonical query model instead of ad hoc page-local sort rules.
notes: This task is intentionally UI-visible but thin in logic. If page code starts duplicating normalization rules, it is a sign that Phases 1-2 were bypassed.

title: Preserve and reset query behavior across search/filter/sort/pagination
description: Wire LibraryListPage interactions so search, category filter, sort changes, and pagination preserve or reset query state exactly according to the canonical semantics and always keep the URL in sync.
design: This task is the behavioral heart of the frontend track. It should make the page feel consistent across every interaction path and ensure the URL remains the single recoverable representation of state.
acceptance_criteria: Changing search/category/sort resets page to 1, moving between pages keeps search/category/sort intact, and reloading a query-string URL reconstructs the same page state.
notes: This is where many subtle regressions tend to hide. Prefer explicit behavior code over clever abstractions so the rules stay obvious to future maintainers.

title: Review and align LibraryCategoryPage with expanded query model
description: Review LibraryCategoryPage and decide whether any alignment changes are needed now that the shared library query model includes sort state and updated semantics.
design: This is a containment task designed to prevent semantic drift between /library and /library/categories/:categorySlug. The output can be either targeted changes or an explicit no-change decision with rationale.
acceptance_criteria: The category page has been reviewed against the expanded query contract, and any required adjustments or intentional exclusions are documented through code/tests/notes.
notes: This task exists because shared query utilities are used outside the main list page. Ignoring that coupling would make future bugs harder to explain.

title: Preserve empty-state, detail links, and page resilience
description: Ensure the Library list still handles empty results, loading/error states, malformed shared-link inputs, and detail-page navigation cleanly after sort behavior is integrated.
design: This is a resilience pass on the list page rather than a redesign. The user should still be able to understand the page state, recover from malformed query strings without a broken experience, and reach /library/drawings/{id} reliably under filtered or empty-result conditions.
acceptance_criteria: Empty-state remains friendly, loading/error behavior is intact, malformed /library query strings do not crash the page, and drawing cards still navigate correctly to detail pages after sort integration.
notes: Optimization pass (2026-03-12): broadened from generic resilience to explicitly protect malformed shared links because that is a real user-facing failure mode for search/filter/sort features.

title: Add LibraryListPage tests for sort, URL persistence, and page reset
description: Expand page-level frontend tests so LibraryListPage behavior is protected for sort control rendering, query-string updates, page reset rules, interaction continuity, and detail navigation.
design: These tests are the user-journey equivalent of the query-utils and API-wrapper contract tests. They should prove that the page stitches the lower-level pieces together correctly.
acceptance_criteria: LibraryListPage tests cover sort control rendering, URL query updates, reset-to-page-1 behavior, persistence of search/category/sort across pagination, safe fallback for malformed shared URLs, and correct detail-page links.
notes: Optimization pass (2026-03-12): page tests should explicitly protect malformed shared-link behavior so the user-facing route remains resilient even while the backend API contract stays strict.

title: Phase 5: QA, verification, and handoff
description: Collect final verification evidence, exercise the manual smoke journey, and leave future contributors with clear notes about residual risks, follow-up opportunities, and the intended semantics of the Search & Filter epic.
design: Closeout track for proving the epic is done, not merely coded. This phase turns implementation into reusable project knowledge by anchoring verification evidence and handoff notes.
acceptance_criteria: Manual smoke expectations are documented, relevant automated verification commands run cleanly, and handoff notes capture what was implemented, what remains intentionally out of scope, and what future extensions must preserve.
notes: This phase exists because search/filter/sort regressions are easy to reintroduce later. Good handoff material reduces that risk across future sessions and parallel agents.

title: Run manual smoke journey for search/filter/sort/detail flow
description: Execute and record the manual smoke journey for the main Library list flow as soon as the key implementation beads are in place, covering search, category filter, sort changes, pagination continuity, URL reload/share behavior, empty results, malformed shared links, and detail-page navigation.
design: Manual smoke exists to verify the actual route-level experience and should run as soon as the implemented behavior is testable in the browser, not only after every automated test bead is complete. This keeps user-feedback discovery earlier in the plan-space loop.
acceptance_criteria: The manual checklist has been exercised against the implemented flow, including malformed shared-link recovery, and any deviations, UX quirks, or follow-up opportunities are captured for future contributors.
notes: Optimization pass (2026-03-12): re-wired to depend on implementation beads instead of automated test beads so manual QA can happen earlier and inform hardening faster. Automated verification still lives in bd-38g.5.2.

title: Run local verification matrix for Library-related suites
description: Run the agreed local verification commands for the Library-related backend and frontend suites so completion is backed by evidence instead of assumption.
design: This task converts the epic from ?looks implemented? to ?verified implemented.? The output should be concise, evidence-oriented, and tied to the exact commands already documented in the PRD/test-spec.
acceptance_criteria: Relevant backend tests, frontend tests, lint/build steps, and any agreed local verification scripts run successfully or produce documented blockers with clear next actions.
notes: The project README already emphasizes evidence-backed completion. This task makes that requirement concrete for the epic.

title: Update handoff, residual-risk, and future-extension notes
description: Capture the final intent, delivered scope, residual risks, and future extension guardrails for the Search & Filter epic so later contributors can build on it without re-litigating core semantics.
design: This documentation task should leave behind enough context for future sessions to understand not just what changed, but why the chosen behavior exists and what must remain stable if the Library module grows.
acceptance_criteria: Handoff material clearly states the delivered semantics, known non-goals, residual risks, and the key invariants future work must preserve when introducing relevance ranking or real persistence.
notes: This is the memory-preservation layer of the epic. Good notes reduce the chance that future work quietly breaks the contract established here.
